editorial.title: 세계에 떠넘긴 호르무즈 통행료, 3000억 달러 재건 비용
editorial.body: (사설) 미국과 이란이 14개 조항의 종전 MOU에 서명하며 전쟁이 종료됐다. 호르무즈 해상 봉쇄 해제, 이란 재건기금 마련, 통행료 문제 등과 관련해 한국 등 국제사회 부담 가능성이 제기된다. |
summary: 미국과 이란이 14개 조항 MOU 서명으로 전쟁 종료.
호르무즈 해상 봉쇄 해제 및 이란 재건에 국제사회 부담.
한국 등이 3000억 달러 통행료·재건 비용 떠안을 수 있어.
editorial.pullQuote: 호르무즈 해상 봉쇄 해제, 이란 재건기금 마련, 통행료 문제 등과 관련해 한국 등 국제사회 부담 가능성이 제기된다.
editorial.stance: 미이란 합의의 후속 비용을 국제사회가 공평하게 분담하는 메커니즘 마련이 필요하다.
editorial.titleTr: World Left Holding the Bill: $300 Billion Strait of Hormuz Toll for Rebuilding
summaryTr: The US and Iran end war with signing of 14-article MOU.
International community bears burden of lifting Hormuz strait blockade and rebuilding Iran.
South Korea and others could shoulder 300 billion dollars in transit fees and reconstruction costs.
editorial.bodyTr: The United States and Iran have signed a 14-point Memorandum of Understanding to end the war. Concerns have been raised about potential burdens on South Korea and the international community regarding the lifting of the Strait of Hormuz blockade, establishing a reconstruction fund for Iran, and resolving toll-related issues.

editorials.titleTr: [Editorial] Joint Investigation Task Force Probe Must Chart Path to Reform Election Commission
summaryTr: The joint investigation team recommended launching investigations into 12 people, including the former chairman of the National Election Commission.
This is a measure to hold the election commission accountable for its systemic failures.
This process should lead to finding answers for reforming the National Election Commission.
editorials.bodyTr:
The Fact-Finding Committee of the National Election Commission recommended on the 19th that investigations be launched into 12 senior officials, including former NEC Chairman Noh Tae-ak. The committee cited the need to hold the NEC accountable for its systemic failures. The Fact-Finding Committee determined that the NEC failed to fulfill its legal responsibilities in multiple areas, including its duty to prevent illegal campaign activities and to respond appropriately to violations.

The joint investigation team's probe should become a process of finding answers to how the NEC needs to be reformed and improved. The NEC's credibility has been severely damaged by a series of controversies involving the presidential election, the National Assembly elections, and local elections. The public's trust in the electoral system itself has been shaken.

The NEC is a constitutional institution responsible for protecting the fairness and integrity of elections. When such an institution loses public confidence, it represents a fundamental crisis for democratic governance. The joint investigation team must thoroughly investigate the causes of the NEC's failures and present concrete measures for institutional reform.

The investigation should not stop at simply assigning blame to individual officials. Rather, it should focus on identifying structural problems within the NEC and proposing systemic improvements. Only through this approach can the NEC regain public trust and fulfill its proper role as guardian of electoral integrity in a democratic society.

editorials.title: [사설] 사전투표 대신 본투표 이틀, 선거불신 해소방안 될 수 있다
editorials.body: 사전투표 대신 본투표 이틀, 선거불신 해소방안 될 수 있다 국민의힘 의원 25명이 사전 투표를 폐지하고 본투표를 이틀로 늘리는 내용의 공직선거법 개정안을 발의했다. 본투표에 참여하지 못하는 유권자는 사전에 신고한 경우 선거일 4일 전부터 이틀간 미리 투표할 수
editorials.pullQuote: 본투표를 이틀로 늘리는 공직선거법 개정안으로 선거불신 해소 방안이 될 수 있다.
summary: 국민의힘 의원 25명이 사전투표 폐지 및 본투표 이틀 확대 법안을 발의했다.
본투표를 이틀로 늘려 더 많은 유권자의 투표 기회를 보장하는 방안이다.
선거 신뢰도 회복을 위한 제도 개선 논의가 시작됐다.
editorials.stance: 본투표 이틀 확대로 투표 접근성을 높이고 선거 신뢰도를 개선하는 긍정적 변화를 평가한다.
editorials.titleTr: [Editorial] Two Days of Early Voting Instead of Election Day Could Help Restore Faith in Elections
summaryTr: Twenty-five lawmakers from the People Power Party have proposed a bill to abolish early voting and extend the main election period to two days.
The plan aims to guarantee voting opportunities for more eligible voters by expanding the main election to two days.
Discussions on institutional reforms to restore trust in elections have begun.
editorials.bodyTr:
Extending Election Day to Two Days Instead of Early Voting Could Be a Solution to Resolve Election Distrust

Twenty-five members of the People Power Party have proposed amendments to the Public Official Election Act that would abolish early voting and extend Election Day to two days. Voters unable to participate on Election Day would be able to vote in advance during a two-day period four days before the election, provided they have submitted prior notification.

leanScore.label: Lean conservative

editorial.title: [사설] 감시 무풍지대 선관위의 끝 모를 도덕적 해이
editorial.body: 입력 2026.06.18 10:33 2026.06.18 09:43 2026.06.18 06:09 노태악 당시 중앙선거관리위원장이 지난 5일 ‘투표용지 부족 사태’ 관련 대국민 사과를 하고 있다. 뉴스1 중앙선거관리위원회가 투표용지 부족 사태를 초래한 6·3 지방선거 당시 일부 투표소에서 극심한 혼란상이 나타났던 것으로 확인되고 있다. 선거 당일 상황을 기록한 투표록에 따르면, 서울 잠실2동 한 투표소에선 투표관리관 도장이 찍히지 않은 채 용지가 배부됐다. 다른 투표소에선 바닥에 투표용지가 떨어져 있거나, 용지 부족 때문에 선관위에 전화를 걸어도 연락이 닿지 않았다고 한다. 추가 투표용지가 도착한 뒤에도 대기표를 받았던 주민들이 돌아오지 않아 참정권 행사가 불발됐다. 지난해 대선 때도 다른 사람의 선거인명부에 서명하고 투표한 사례가 2000건이 넘는 등 선거 관리 전반의 부실이 심각하다. 다 이유가 있었다. 선거로 업무가 바빠지는 해에 휴직자가 오히려 늘어나는 게 이번뿐 아니라 선관위의 전통이라고 한다. 이 조직이 공정한 선거 관리를 맡을 자격이 있는지 의문을 갖게 한다. 문제는 부실에서 그치지 않는다. 선관위 지도부부터 심각한 도덕적 해이에 빠져 있었다는 사실이 속속 드러나고 있다. 노태악 전 중앙선관위원장은 지난해 덴마크·스웨덴을 비롯해 재임 중 세 차례 간 해외 출장에 모두 배우자를 동반했다. 1200만원이 넘는 비즈니스 항공권 등의 경비를 선관위가 지불했다. 공식적인 외교나 국가 중대사도 아닌 해외 선거 관련 방문에 세금을 흥청망청 쓴 것이어서 외유성 출장이라는 비판을 면키 어렵다. 외부에 공개되는 출장 보고서에 ‘부부 동반’ 문구를 넣지 않은 것만 봐도 명백한 특혜가 아닐 수 없다. 기관장이 이러니 직원들의 일탈은 예고된 것이나 마찬가지였다. 선관위 직원들은 몰디브 등 세계적인 휴양지로 출장을 가고 선거 홍보물로 활용된다며 해변 사진을 버젓이 보고서에 첨부했다. 역량 강화를 이유로 이탈리아 등 관광지에도 매년 직원을 보냈다. 심지어 선관위 고위직들의 ‘자녀 특혜 채용’ 의혹이 불거진 시기에도 외유성 출장이 계속될 정도로 내부 기강 해이가 심각하다. 독립성을 방패 삼아 외부의 감시와 견제를 피해 성역을 구축해 온 선관위는 전면적인 개혁 없이는 국민의 신뢰를 회복할 수 없는 지경에 이르렀다. 예산 집행의 투명성 확보와 함께 채용 비리를 차단할 감시 시스템이 도입돼야 한다. 신뢰를 잃은 선관위가 관리하는 선거는 결과에 대한 불신의 씨앗을 남길 우려가 있다.

editorial.titleTr: Editorial: The Election Commission's Bottomless Moral Hazard in the Surveillance Gray Zone
summaryTr: Severe chaos erupted during the June 3 local elections due to ballot shortages, revealing serious negligence in election commission management
Former Chairman Noh Tae-ak wasted over 12 million won in taxpayer money on overseas business trips accompanied by his spouse
The election commission has evaded oversight by using independence as a shield, and trust cannot be restored without comprehensive reform
editorial.bodyTr: The National Election Commission has confirmed that severe chaos occurred at some polling places during the June 3 local elections that led to the ballot shortage crisis. According to voting records documenting the situation on election day, at one polling station in Jamsil 2-dong, Seoul, ballots were distributed without the election supervisor's stamp. At other polling stations, ballots were found scattered on the floor, and residents reported that calls to the election commission went unanswered due to ballot shortages. Even after additional ballots arrived, residents who had received waiting tickets did not return, resulting in lost voting opportunities. During last year's presidential election, there were over 2,000 cases of people signing other voters' names and casting ballots, indicating serious mismanagement across the entire election administration system. There were reasons for this. According to reports, it is a tradition at the election commission that the number of employees on leave actually increases during years when election work becomes busy. This raises questions about whether this organization is qualified to manage fair elections. The problem extends beyond mere negligence. It is becoming increasingly clear that the election commission's leadership has fallen into serious ethical lapses. Former Election Commission Chairman Roh Tae-ak accompanied his spouse on all three overseas business trips during his tenure, including visits to Denmark and Sweden. The election commission paid for business-class airfare exceeding 12 million won and other expenses. Using taxpayer money for overseas visits related to elections that were neither official diplomatic missions nor major state affairs is difficult to defend against criticism of junket travel. The fact that official trip reports did not mention "accompanied by spouse" makes clear that this was outright favoritism. Given the conduct of the agency head, employee misconduct was virtually inevitable. Election commission staff went on business trips to world-renowned vacation destinations like the Maldives and brazenly attached beach photos to reports, claiming they would be used in election promotional materials. They sent employees yearly to tourist destinations like Italy under the guise of capacity building. Internal discipline has deteriorated so severely that even when suspicions arose of "nepotistic hiring" among senior election commission officials, junket travel continued unabated. The election commission, using independence as a shield to avoid external oversight and checks while creating a sanctuary, has reached a point where it cannot recover public trust without comprehensive reform. Transparency in budget execution must be secured, and a monitoring system to prevent hiring irregularities must be introduced. Elections managed by an election commission that has lost public trust risk leaving seeds of doubt about their outcomes.

leanScore.label: Lean progressive
leanScore.rationale: 공공기관의 도덕적 해이와 특권, 투명성 부족을 강하게 비판하며 감시 체계 강화를 요구하는 진보적 프레이밍으로, 권력 기관의 부실과 기강 해이에 대한 진보진영의 전형적 관점을 드러냄.

editorial.title: Seasonal stalemate
editorial.body: Korea must rethink minimum wage politics for a changing economy Every summer, South Korea performs a ritual as predictable as the monsoon. Labor and business representatives gather, exchange familiar accusations and eventually settle on a compromise that satisfies neither side. The choreography rarely changes. The economy beneath it, however, has changed profoundly. Organized labor has proposed raising next year's minimum wage by 16.3 percent to 12,000 won ($7.90) an hour. Employers are again demanding industry-specific rates, arguing that a single nationwide standard no longer reflects reality. The debate sounds familiar; the underlying economy does not. South Korea's minimum wage, now 10,320 won, already ranks among the highest in East Asia. Yet the annual dispute has become more than an argument over purchasing power and business costs. It increasingly exposes an economy split between globally competitive industries and domestic sectors struggling to remain viable. Workers have legitimate grievances. Average minimum wage increases over the past three years stood at 2.37 percent, below the average inflation rate of 2.66 percent. Real incomes for low-wage earners have therefore lost ground. Semiconductor windfalls and widening asset inequality have reinforced perceptions of a two-speed economy. Against that backdrop, an hourly wage that barely covers the cost of a simple lunch in Seoul has become a tangible measure of how unevenly prosperity is distributed. The same two-speed economy that frustrates workers also shapes employers' capacity to pay. According to employer groups, value added per worker in accommodation and food services is around 28 million won, barely one-sixth of manufacturing's 170 million won. The strain is already visible. More than 31 percent of businesses in accommodation and food services failed to meet the statutory minimum wage last year. That suggests less a disregard for the law than an inability to comply. In those industries, the minimum wage already amounts to roughly 87 percent of the median wage, leaving little room to absorb additional costs. The consequences extend beyond corporate balance sheets. Faced with mounting labor expenses, many small businesses are turning to kiosks, self-service systems and robots. Entry-level jobs often disappear first. The timing could hardly be worse. Permanent employment recently declined for the first time in more than 26 years, with much of the weakness concentrated among workers in their 20s and 30s. More than 500,000 university graduates are unemployed, a level not seen since the pandemic years. Public finances are hardly immune. Because unemployment benefits are pegged to the minimum wage, sharp increases in the statutory wage floor feed directly into welfare spending. Last year's jobless benefits reached a record 17.5 trillion won, while the employment insurance fund has fallen deep into deficit. The system itself has struggled to adapt. The Minimum Wage Act has permitted industry-specific rates since 1988, but the provision has been used only once. Labor groups fear differentiated wages would stigmatize certain occupations and worsen labor shortages. Those concerns deserve respect. Yet mechanical equality can produce distortions of its own when economic conditions vary so widely. A nation that prides itself on technological sophistication should not assume that identical rules automatically guarantee fairness. Neither freezing wages nor imposing large increases offers a durable answer. A more sensible approach would be to start pilot programs in the most vulnerable sectors, accompanied by targeted tax relief, productivity-enhancing support for microbusinesses and welfare reforms less dependent on the minimum wage itself. Every summer brings another season of familiar arguments. Rituals have their place, but they are poor substitutes for adaptation. South Korea’s challenge is no longer choosing between labor and management. It is adapting institutions to an economy of widening disparities, while recognizing that social protection cannot rest indefinitely on the payrolls of private employers. summary: South Korea's annual minimum wage ritual ignores a two-speed economy split between thriving tech sectors and struggling domestic businesses.

The editorial urges pilot programs and targeted support instead of mechanical equality that ignores vastly different sector economics.
editorial.stance: One-size-fits-all minimum wage policy must adapt to Korea's unequal economy through sector-specific pilots and structural reforms.
summaryTr: 한국의 연간 최저임금 결정 과정은 번영하는 기술 부문과 어려움을 겪는 국내 기업 사이에 양극화된 이중 경제 구조를 외면하고 있다.
노동계는 16.3% 임금 인상을 요구하고 있는 반면 경영진은 이를 따르기 어려워하고 있으며, 이미 외식업 기업의 31%가 현재 최저임금을 충족하지 못하고 있다.
사설은 광대한 부문별 경제 격차를 무시하는 기계적 평등 대신 시범 사업과 맞춤형 지원을 촉구하고 있다.
editorial.bodyTr:
한국은 변화하는 경제에 맞춰 최저임금 정책을 재검토해야 한다

매년 여름 한국은 몬순만큼 예측 가능한 의식을 치른다. 노사 대표들이 모여 익숙한 비난을 주고받다가 결국 어느 쪽도 만족하지 않는 타협에 도달한다. 이 과정의 구성은 거의 변하지 않는다. 그러나 그 아래에 있는 경제는 깊이 있게 변했다. 노동조합은 내년 최저임금을 시간당 12,000원(7.90달러)으로 16.3% 인상할 것을 제안했다. 고용주들은 다시 한 번 업종별 요금을 요구하면서, 단일한 전국 기준이 더 이상 현실을 반영하지 않는다고 주장하고 있다. 이 논쟁은 익숙해 보이지만, 그 아래의 경제는 그렇지 않다. 현재 10,320원인 한국의 최저임금은 이미 동아시아에서 가장 높은 수준에 있다. 그렇지만 연례 분쟁은 구매력과 사업 비용을 둘러싼 논쟁 이상이 되어버렸다. 이는 갈수록 글로벌 경쟁력 있는 산업과 생존 가능성을 유지하기 위해 고군분투하는 국내 부문 사이에 갈라진 경제를 드러내고 있다.

근로자들의 불만은 정당하다. 지난 3년간 최저임금 평균 인상률은 2.37%로, 평균 물가상승률 2.66%를 하회했다. 저임금 근로자의 실질 소득은 따라서 감소했다. 반도체 호황과 자산 불평등 심화는 이중 속도 경제에 대한 인식을 강화했다. 이러한 배경 속에서 서울에서 간단한 점심을 먹을 수 있는 비용도 겨우 충당하는 시간급은 번영이 얼마나 불고르게 분배되고 있는지를 보여주는 구체적인 척도가 되었다.

근로자들을 좌절시키는 같은 이중 속도 경제가 고용주들의 임금 지불 능력도 좌우한다. 고용주 단체에 따르면, 숙박 및 식음료 산업의 근로자당 부가가치는 약 2,800만원으로, 제조업의 1억7,000만원의 6분의 1도 채 되지 않는다. 그 부담은 이미 드러나고 있다. 숙박 및 식음료 산업의 31% 이상이 지난해 법정 최저임금을 충족하지 못했다. 이는 법을 무시하려는 태도라기보다 준수할 수 없는 상황을 시사한다. 그 산업들에서 최저임금은 이미 중위 임금의 약 87%에 달해, 추가 비용을 흡수할 여지가 거의 없다.

그 결과는 기업 재무 현황을 넘어선다. 증가하는 인건비에 직면한 많은 소규모 업체들은 키오스크, 셀프서비스 시스템, 로봇으로 눈을 돌리고 있다. 초급 일자리들이 가장 먼저 사라진다. 이 시점이 더 나쁠 수 없다. 상용직 고용이 26년 이상 처음으로 감소했으며, 약점의 대부분은 20대와 30대 근로자들에게 집중되어 있다. 50만 명이 넘는 대학 졸업자들이 실업 상태에 있으며, 이는 팬데믹 이후 보지 못한 수준이다.

정부 재정도 면역이 아니다. 실업 급여가 최저임금에 연동되어 있기 때문에, 법정 임금 하한선의 급격한 인상은 복지 지출로 직결된다. 지난해 실업급여는 17조5,000억원의 사상 최고치에 달했고, 고용보험기금은 깊은 적자에 빠졌다. 체계 자체가 적응하기 위해 애썼다. 최저임금법은 1988년 이후 업종별 요금을 허용했으나, 이 조항은 단 한 번만 사용되었다. 노동 단체들은 차등 임금이 특정 직업을 낙인찍고 노동 부족을 악화시킬까봐 우려한다. 그러한 우려는 존중받을 가치가 있다. 그럼에도 기계적 평등은 경제 여건이 매우 다를 때 그 자체로 왜곡을 초래할 수 있다. 기술 정교함을 자랑하는 국가가 동일한 규칙이 자동으로 공정함을 보장한다고 가정해서는 안 된다.

임금을 동결하거나 큰 인상을 강행하는 것도 지속 가능한 답이 될 수 없다. 더욱 현명한 접근은 가장 취약한 부문에서 시범 프로그램을 시작하되, 목표화된 세제 감면, 영세 사업체의 생산성 향상 지원, 최저임금 자체에 덜 의존하는 복지 개혁을 동반하는 것이다. 매년 여름은 익숙한 논쟁의 또 다른 시즌을 가져온다. 의식은 제 자리를 가지고 있지만, 그것들은 적응의 대체재로는 형편없다. 한국의 과제는 더 이상 노동과 경영 사이에서 선택하는 것이 아니다. 그것은 불평등이 확대되는 경제에 제도를 적응시키면서, 사회 보호가 민간 고용주의 급여에 무한정 의존할 수는 없다는 점을 인식하는 것이다.

leanScore.label: Center
leanScore.rationale: The editorial systematically validates both workers' real wage losses and employers' genuine inability to pay in vulnerable sectors, then proposes pragmatic reforms (pilot programs, tax relief, welfare reform) rather than advocating for either labor or capital.

editorials.title: Seasonal stalemate
editorials.body: Korea must rethink minimum wage politics for a changing economy Every summer, South Korea performs a ritual as predictable as the monsoon. Labor and business representatives gather, exchange familiar accusations and eventually settle on a compromise that satisfies neither side. The choreography rarely changes. The economy beneath it, however, has changed profoundly. Organized labor has proposed raising next year's minimum wage by 16.3 percent to 12,000 won ($7.90) an hour. Employers are again demanding industry-specific rates, arguing that a single nationwide standard no longer reflects reality. The debate sounds familiar; the underlying economy does not. South Korea's minimum wage, now 10,320 won, already ranks among the highest in East Asia. Yet the annual dispute has become more than an argument over purchasing power and business costs. It increasingly exposes an economy split between globally competitive industries and domestic sectors struggling to remain viable. Workers have legitimate grievances. Average minimum wage increases over the past three years stood at 2.37 percent, below the average inflation rate of 2.66 percent. Real incomes for low-wage earners have therefore lost ground. Semiconductor windfalls and widening asset inequality have reinforced perceptions of a two-speed economy. Against that backdrop, an hourly wage that barely covers the cost of a simple lunch in Seoul has become a tangible measure of how unevenly prosperity is distributed. The same two-speed economy that frustrates workers also shapes employers' capacity to pay. According to employer groups, value added per worker in accommodation and food services is around 28 million won, barely one-sixth of manufacturing's 170 million won. The strain is already visible. More than 31 percent of businesses in accommodation and food services failed to meet the statutory minimum wage last year. That suggests less a disregard for the law than an inability to comply. In those industries, the minimum wage already amounts to roughly 87 percent of the median wage, leaving little room to absorb additional costs. The consequences extend beyond corporate balance sheets. Faced with mounting labor expenses, many small businesses are turning to kiosks, self-service systems and robots. Entry-level jobs often disappear first. The timing could hardly be worse. Permanent employment recently declined for the first time in more than 26 years, with much of the weakness concentrated among workers in their 20s and 30s. More than 500,000 university graduates are unemployed, a level not seen since the pandemic years. Public finances are hardly immune. Because unemployment benefits are pegged to the minimum wage, sharp increases in the statutory wage floor feed directly into welfare spending. Last year's jobless benefits reached a record 17.5 trillion won, while the employment insurance fund has fallen deep into deficit. The system itself has struggled to adapt. The Minimum Wage Act has permitted industry-specific rates since 1988, but the provision has been used only once. Labor groups fear differentiated wages would stigmatize certain occupations and worsen labor shortages. Those concerns deserve respect. Yet mechanical equality can produce distortions of its own when economic conditions vary so widely. A nation that prides itself on technological sophistication should not assume that identical rules automatically guarantee fairness. Neither freezing wages nor imposing large increases offers a durable answer. A more sensible approach would be to start pilot programs in the most vulnerable sectors, accompanied by targeted tax relief, productivity-enhancing support for microbusinesses and welfare reforms less dependent on the minimum wage itself. Every summer brings another season of familiar arguments. Rituals have their place, but they are poor substitutes for adaptation. South Korea’s challenge is no longer choosing between labor and management. It is adapting institutions to an economy of widening disparities, while recognizing that social protection cannot rest indefinitely on the payrolls of private employers. editorials.stance: One-size-fits-all minimum wage policy must adapt to Korea's unequal economy through sector-specific pilots and structural reforms.
summaryTr: 한국의 연간 최저임금 결정 과정은 번영하는 기술 부문과 어려움을 겪는 국내 기업 사이에 양극화된 이중 경제 구조를 외면하고 있다.
노동계는 16.3% 임금 인상을 요구하고 있는 반면 경영진은 이를 따르기 어려워하고 있으며, 이미 외식업 기업의 31%가 현재 최저임금을 충족하지 못하고 있다.
사설은 광대한 부문별 경제 격차를 무시하는 기계적 평등 대신 시범 사업과 맞춤형 지원을 촉구하고 있다.

editorial.title: [사설] 포용·책임 강조한 이 대통령 회견, 여권 모두 새기고 실천해야
editorial.body: 이재명 대통령이 19일 청와대 춘추관에서 8박9일간의 유럽 순방 및 주요 7개국(G7) 정상회의(G7) 참석 결과를 직접 브리핑했다. 이 대통령은 이날 회견에서 도널드 트럼프 미국 대통령과 레오 14세 교황을 비롯해 주요국 정상들과 나눈 대화 내용 등을 비교적 상세히
editorial.pullQuote: 이 대통령은 이날 회견에서 도널드 트럼프 미국 대통령과 레오 14세 교황을 비롯해 주요국 정상들과 나눈 대화 내용을 브리핑했다.
summary: 이재명 대통령이 유럽 순방과 G7 정상회의 결과를 브리핑했다.
트럼프 대통령과 교황 등 주요 정상들과의 대화 내용을 공개했다.
포용과 책임을 강조하며 여권의 실천을 촉구했다.

editorial.titleTr: [Editorial] President Lee's Press Conference Emphasizing Inclusivity and Accountability: Ruling Camp Must Heed and Act on It
summaryTr: President Lee Jae-myung briefed on the results of his European tour and the G7 summit.
He disclosed details of conversations with President Trump, the Pope, and other major world leaders.
He emphasized inclusivity and responsibility while urging the ruling party to put them into practice.
editorial.bodyTr: President Lee Jae-myung held a direct briefing on the 19th at the Cheong Wa Dae press center regarding results from his eight-day, nine-night European tour and attendance at the Group of Seven summit. During the press conference that day, President Lee provided relatively detailed accounts of conversations held with U.S. President Donald Trump, Pope Leo XIV, and other major world leaders.

editorials.titleTr: Editorial: Korea's Weekly Apartment Price Statistics Need Reassessment
summaryTr: The head of Korea Real Estate Board publicly pointed out problems with the weekly apartment price statistics announcement.
Many questions are being raised both internally and externally about the significance of weekly statistics.
South Korea's unprecedented global practice of releasing weekly statistics by the government should be reconsidered.
editorials.bodyTr: Lee Heon-uk, director of the Korea Real Estate Board, has publicly expressed negative views on weekly releases of apartment prices. At a press briefing on the 18th, Director Lee said, "I understand there have been many questions raised internally and externally about the statistical significance of weekly apartment price trends," and added that "globally as well, official government statistics on apartment prices are typically released on a monthly or quarterly basis.

editorial.title: 현실화하는 '호르무즈 통행료'… 국가간 연대로 막아야
editorial.body: 미국과 이란이 종전 양해각서(MOU)에 서명했지만 이를 바라보는 세계의 시선은 안도보다는 착잡함에 가깝다. 이란 핵 문제의 불투명성은 전쟁 이전과 달라진 것이 없고, 최소 3000억달러(약 457조원)로 명시된 이란 재건·경제개발 기금의 부담 주체도 불명확하다. MOU에는 '역내 파트너들과 계획을 마련한다'고 표현됐지만 한국 등 호르무즈 해협에 이해가 걸린..
editorial.pullQuote: MOU에는 '역내 파트너들과 계획을 마련한다'고 표현됐지만 한국 등 호르무즈 해협에 이해가 걸린...
summary: 미국-이란 양해각서 체결했으나 핵 문제 투명성 여전히 불투명.
3000억달러 이란 재건 기금 부담 주체가 명확하지 않음.
호르무즈 해협 이해국들의 국가간 연대로 대응 필요.

editorial.titleTr: Looming "Hormuz Transit Tax" Demands International Unity to Block
summaryTr: U.S.-Iran memorandum of understanding signed, but nuclear issue transparency remains unclear.
The entity responsible for funding Iran's $300 billion reconstruction project is not clearly defined.
Countries with interests in the Strait of Hormuz need to respond through international cooperation.
editorial.bodyTr: The United States and Iran have signed a memorandum of understanding on ending hostilities, yet the world's view of it leans more toward unease than relief. The opacity surrounding Iran's nuclear issue remains unchanged from before the war, and the burden-bearer for Iran's reconstruction and economic development fund, specified at a minimum of 300 billion dollars, remains unclear. The MOU states that "plans will be established with regional partners," but countries like South Korea with stakes in the Strait of Hormuz...

editorials.titleTr: Intel Poaches SK Hynix CEO, Signaling Fierce Competition for Korean Chip Talent Abroad
summaryTr: Intel has recruited Lee Seok-hee, former president of SK Hynix, as senior vice president.
The global advancement of Korean semiconductor talent demonstrates the rising status of K-semiconductors.
The industry urgently needs to prepare countermeasures against threats of technology and talent outflow.
editorials.bodyTr: American semiconductor giant Intel has brought on Lee Seok-hee, former president of SK Hynix, as senior vice president. The appointment of a top executive from Korea's leading semiconductor company to the upper echelons of a global tech powerhouse demonstrates the elevated standing of Korean semiconductors on the global stage. At the same time, it raises a weighty question about whether the industry is adequately prepared in the face of intensifying global talent competition and threats of technology leakage. According to Intel's announcement, the former president will lead the foundry business...

editorials.titleTr: Two of Three Jobless Benefits Recipients Exhaust Payments ... Need to Strengthen Work Incentives
summaryTr: Two out of every three unemployment benefit recipients receive the full amount until expiration.
Complete receipt until expiration far outnumbers reemployment during benefit periods.
Urgent institutional reform is needed to address the problem of declining job-seeking motivation.
editorials.bodyTr: Unemployment benefits are facing criticism for dampening job-seeking motivation, contrary to their original purpose of helping the jobless make a swift comeback. This is because the vast majority of recipients exhaust their full benefit period without finding reemployment, rather than securing new jobs while still receiving payments. Given this situation, it is inevitable that the employment insurance fund, which finances unemployment benefits, will face depletion.
